primary_diagnosis.name: ACE inhibitor-induced (bradykinin-mediated) angioedema
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Angioedema caused by angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor

pathophysiology_summary: ACE inhibitors reduce bradykinin breakdown; accumulated bradykinin increases vascular permeability, causing deep dermal/subcutaneous non-pitting, non-urticarial, often asymmetric angioedema of distensible tissues (lips, face, tongue, larynx). Onset can be days to years after starting the drug (here ~10 days). Because it is bradykinin- (not histamine/mast-cell) mediated, adrenaline, corticosteroids and antihistamines are largely ineffective — the mainstays are ceasing the ACE inhibitor, airway monitoring, and in severe/laryngeal cases hospital therapies (e.g., icatibant/C1-esterase inhibitor) and airway management. Anaphylaxis is excluded by the absence of urticaria, wheeze, hypotension and GI symptoms, but this does NOT reduce airway vigilance. The critical telehealth limitation is that the oropharynx/larynx cannot be examined remotely, so airway patency cannot be assured — mandating emergency in-person assessment given rapid progression and distance from hospital.

differential.evidence_basis: Deep non-itchy asymmetric facial/lip swelling ~10 days after starting perindopril, no urticaria.
Any acute facial/airway swelling must be assessed for anaphylaxis; here no urticaria/wheeze/hypotension/GI symptoms.
Hyper-acute (hours) onset, afebrile, no demarcated hot spreading plaque — against cellulitis.
No pruritus, weeping or epidermal change; swelling is deep and subcutaneous.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Identify the ACE-inhibitor link and screen the airway; do NOT falsely reassure that antihistamines will fix it, and do NOT downgrade vigilance just because it is 'not anaphylaxis'.

differential.evidence_basis: Bradykinin pattern: deep, tense, non-urticarial, asymmetric, ACE-inhibitor exposure; airway currently patent but at risk.
No urticaria, wheeze, hypotension or GI symptoms — not anaphylaxis (but airway vigilance maintained).
Hyper-acute, afebrile, no demarcated hot plaque.

differential.diagnosis: ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema — airway at risk
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis: cease ACE inhibitor and obtain emergency in-person assessment for airway monitoring; standard allergy drugs are unreliable.

red_flags.description: Recent ACE inhibitor initiation with rapidly progressive angioedema
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifies bradykinin-mediated angioedema that is poorly responsive to standard allergy treatment and can progress to the airway.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continuing the ACE inhibitor and relying on antihistamines risks progression to laryngeal oedema and asphyxiation.

red_flags.description: Any airway symptom: voice change, throat tightness/lump, difficulty swallowing saliva, drooling, or stridor
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals laryngeal involvement — an immediate life threat requiring 000 and hospital airway management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early laryngeal oedema can progress to fatal airway obstruction, especially 30 minutes from hospital.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Concluding it is 'just angioedema, not anaphylaxis' and downgrading urgency, missing the airway risk.
Relying on antihistamines/steroids/adrenaline, which are largely ineffective in ACE-inhibitor angioedema.
Agreeing to observe at home when the airway cannot be examined remotely and swelling is progressing.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Oropharyngeal/laryngeal examination to confirm airway patency
Objective monitoring of swelling progression and vitals over time
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Cease the ACE inhibitor now and arrange emergency in-person assessment (ambulance if any airway symptom or rapid progression), because airway safety cannot be verified remotely and the patient is ~30 minutes from hospital.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring and observing at home, or relying on antihistamines, risks fatal airway obstruction from progressing laryngeal oedema.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: For ACE-inhibitor angioedema: cease the ACE inhibitor permanently, monitor the airway closely, and manage severe/laryngeal cases in hospital (adrenaline/steroids/antihistamines are unreliable; consider icatibant/C1-INH). Any airway symptom mandates emergency care.
